evals.prompt: Build a value case for an H3 bet on AI-native commerce. The CFO wants 'the NPV.' What do you give him?
evals.expected_output: Should refuse to compute traditional NPV for H3 work and reframe the conversation. Output: a real-options brief, not an NPV. Structure: (1) Reframe — this is an option on a future business, not a discounted-cash-flow business case; (2) Option premium — the staged discovery investment (typical: $50K-$500K Gate 0-1, $1-3M Gate 2, $5-15M to launch); (3) Strike price — capital required to scale if option exercises; (4) Upside — if the bet works, the addressable market and capture; (5) Decision question — is upside ≥10x discovery cost? Is discovery cost small enough to lose? (6) Strategic option value beyond cash — capability built, market presence, defensive value (cost of being late if competitor wins this category first). Should provide language for the CFO conversation: 'Traditional NPV systematically underweights H3 bets because cash flows are unknowable in year one. The right math is real options. Here's the option case.' Should hand back to portfolio-architect with a recommendation to fund Gate 0-1 and re-evaluate at next gate.

evals.prompt: Compute kill economics for these three pilots. Each has spent $1.2M to date over 9 months. Pilot A: B2B compliance dashboard, ~30% adoption, no clear pricing. Pilot B: AI-assisted advisor matching, strong adoption (~70%), pricing unvalidated. Pilot C: Co-branded ecosystem app, low adoption (~12%), no champion at partner. We need to decide what to fund through Gate 3.
evals.expected_output: Should compute kill economics per pilot: residual value retained if killed, vs. additional spend to advance. Pilot A: spent $1.2M; residual ~$300K (codebase, validated personas, compliance learning, customer relationships). Recommendation: KILL or pivot to specific high-WTP segment; current pricing fog is a Stage 4 (Business Model) failure. Pilot B: spent $1.2M; residual ~$400K but main risk is pricing — recommend ADVANCE to Gate 3 conditional on monetization-strategist running WTP study in next 30 days; without WTP, this is a future minivation. Pilot C: spent $1.2M; residual ~$200K (some IP, partner relationship); 12% adoption + no champion = clear KILL, this is undead pattern. Total kill capital recovered: $2.4M; one pilot advances. Note the pattern: 9 months in, 2 of 3 should kill — that's healthy gate discipline, not failure. Recommend kill ceremonies and learning capture for A and C.

evals.prompt: Roll up the expected portfolio value for this 18-bet portfolio across H1/H2/H3. Show me which bets dominate.
evals.expected_output: Should structure rollup by horizon with appropriate value math: H1 (NPV with sensitivity), H2 (E[V] across bear/base/bull scenarios with risk-adjusted discount), H3 (option value × probability of exercise + strategic option value). Output table: per-bet expected value, % of portfolio total, risk-adjusted contribution, capital deployed, capital efficiency (E[V]/capital). Identify the 'dominators' — typically 2-3 bets account for 60%+ of portfolio expected value (Pareto pattern, holds in most innovation portfolios). Recommend implications: (1) protect dominator bets from cuts during budget cycles; (2) ensure named sponsorship for dominators is robust; (3) the long tail of low-E[V] bets are candidates for kill or merger; (4) capital efficiency comparisons surface where additional investment would compound vs. dilute. Caveat: this analysis is sensitive to scenario probabilities — pressure-test the probability assumptions before committing decisions.

evals.prompt: We're being asked to justify $5M of innovation spend that has produced no revenue in two years. Build the learning-ROI defense.
evals.expected_output: Should reframe the conversation from revenue-ROI to learning-ROI for early-stage portfolios. Structure: (1) The portfolio's actual job — buying optionality and capability, not direct revenue (year 2 H3 work shouldn't show revenue); (2) Learning ROI metrics — $ spent ÷ # critical assumptions tested (target: ~$25-50K per validated assumption), $ spent ÷ # invalidated hypotheses (invalidations save future bad spend; quantify 'avoided-cost' value), time-to-invalidation; (3) Capability built — talent, IP, customer relationships, partnerships, brand authority — quantify each in $; (4) Market presence and strategic option value — what positions did we secure that we couldn't buy now at any price?; (5) Bets advanced through gates — pipeline value, not realized revenue; (6) Comparable benchmarks — corporate venture / R&D portfolios in years 1-2. Should produce a counter-narrative: '$5M bought us [X] validated insights, [Y] killed assumptions saving ~$Z in future bad spend, [N] capabilities, and [M] H2/H3 bets advanced. Revenue is a year-3 metric.' Note: if the innovation work is actually H1 dressed as H3, the learning-ROI defense doesn't hold — be honest about what's real.

evals.prompt: CFO is killing every H3 bet on NPV grounds. Build the real-options brief that reframes the conversation.
evals.expected_output: Should produce a CFO-ready brief that translates H3 work into language CFOs accept. Structure: (1) Open with acknowledgment — 'Traditional NPV systematically underweights uncertain bets; this is documented in McGrath's Real Options literature and standard corporate-finance texts'; (2) The structural problem — H3 cash flows have wide variance, are time-distant, and depend on conditions that don't yet exist; NPV at standard discount rate gives systematically negative values for any option not yet exercised; (3) The reframe — discovery investment is buying an option, not a business. Apply Black-Scholes intuition: low strike (cheap to scale if exercised) + high underlying value (if it works) + high volatility = positive option value even when NPV is negative; (4) Staged commitment — show the kill economics at each gate, prove the at-risk capital is small; (5) Strategic options the company already implicitly buys — R&D portfolios, brand investments, M&A pipeline activity; H3 work is the same shape; (6) Healthy comparison — 70% of long-run portfolio value comes from the 10% disruptive zone (Nagji & Tuff data). Recommend the brief is delivered in person with a paired demo (impression amplifier).

evals.prompt: Compute risk-adjusted NPV for an H2 bet: launching a managed-services tier on top of our composable DXP product line. Year 1: $3M development cost. Years 2-5: revenue ramping from $2M to $18M, gross margin 55%, operating cost 30% of revenue. WACC 10%.
evals.expected_output: Should compute base-case NPV, then adjust for H2 risk (typically WACC + 5-10% additional discount). Base case: Y0 -$3M, Y1 $2M revenue × 25% net contribution = $0.5M, Y2 $6M × 25% = $1.5M, Y3 $12M × 25% = $3M, Y4 $18M × 25% = $4.5M. NPV at 10% ≈ $4.7M. Risk-adjusted at 17.5%: NPV ≈ $1.2M. Should pressure-test assumptions: revenue ramp (Y1-Y2 doubles, Y2-Y3 doubles — is that realistic for managed services?), gross margin (55% is healthy for managed services, but does it survive scale?), customer acquisition cost not modeled — surface as gap; cannibalization not modeled — does this offering steal from existing services? Should produce 3 scenarios: bear ($0M NPV at risk-adjusted), base ($1.2M), bull ($5M). Decision: marginal at risk-adjusted base case. Recommend (a) tighten WTP via monetization-strategist before commit, (b) validate ramp assumptions with 2-3 reference customers, (c) reduce Y0 cost by phasing — pilot tier 1 customers before full build.

evals.prompt: What's the right value math for early-stage discovery work where revenue is impossible to forecast?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend learning ROI as the primary metric for Gate 0-1 work. Structure: (1) $ per validated assumption — track dollars spent vs. number of critical assumptions tested with rigor; healthy range $25-50K per assumption tested for B2B, less for B2C; (2) $ per invalidated hypothesis — invalidations save future bad spend; quantify avoided-cost value (if we'd built the thing, would have spent $X — invalidating saves $X); (3) Time to invalidation — median days from hypothesis to actionable result; target <14 days for early stage; (4) Validated learning produced — count of newly-confirmed customer truths; (5) Pivots informed — the discovery-to-pivot ratio; (6) Capability built — interview-skill maturity, JTBD insight depth. Should NOT compute NPV or revenue projections at this stage. Should produce a 1-pager: dollars in, learning out, decisions enabled. Note: this is the metric that gets H3 work past CFO scrutiny in years 1-2 when revenue is structurally unavailable.

evals.prompt: We're considering acquiring a startup for $40M to accelerate our H3 bet on AI-native onboarding. Should we?
evals.expected_output: Should run an acquire-vs-build value comparison with optionality framing. Structure: (1) Build path — staged investment ($500K discovery, $2-3M alpha/MVP, $5-15M scale) over 18-24 months; option value during build is real but execution risk material; (2) Buy path — $40M for working product + team + customer base + speed to market; option already exercised by seller; (3) Compare on speed-of-impact ($40M for 6-12 months saved vs. self-build risk), capability gained (talent that's hard to recruit at any price), customer access (validated personas, signed contracts), strategic option value (denying competitor the asset); (4) Risk factors — integration failure (Christensen RPV pattern: acquiring companies into the mother ship destroys what made them work), retention of key talent post-acquisition, pricing realization on acquired customers; (5) Recommendation framework — if the strategic option value of speed/talent/access ≥ $40M minus build cost, and integration risk can be mitigated by keeping the unit separate (Christensen separate-unit pattern), acquire. Else build. Hand off integration architecture to innovation-lab-architect (as separate unit) and to consulting-management-consultant for the M&A engagement.
